perturbation.original_text: Company and BUYER mutually agree they may disclose such Confidential Information to their respective responsible employees with a bona fide need to know, and Company and BUYER agree to instruct all such employees not to disclose such Confidential Information to third parties and will ensure that such employees have agreed to similar non-disclosure provisions with Company or the BUYER, its own employees respectively.
perturbation.changed_text: Company and BUYER mutually agree they may disclose such Confidential Information to their respective employees, regardless of need to know, and Company and BUYER agree to instruct all such employees not to disclose such Confidential Information to third parties and will ensure that such employees have agreed to similar non-disclosure provisions with Company or the BUYER, its own employees respectively.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'bona fide need to know' clause broadens the scope of permissible disclosure within the company, conflicting with the general intent of confidentiality agreements to limit access to confidential information. This creates uncertainty about who is authorized to access the data.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification allows disclosure to employees without a 'bona fide need to know,' potentially increasing the risk of misappropriation. The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) protects trade secrets and misappropriation involves unauthorized disclosure. By removing the 'need to know' requirement, the contract weakens the protection of confidential information and potentially contradicts CUTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text broadens the permissible disclosure of confidential information by removing the 'bona fide need to know' requirement. This directly contradicts the intent to protect trade secrets, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code 3426.1, defines 'misappropriation' as the 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent'. By allowing disclosure to employees without a 'need to know', the modified contract increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure and thus misappropriation, creating a contradiction with CUTSA's aim to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Information shall not be deemed Confidential Information and the receiving party shall have no obligation regarding any information for which it can be proven in written documentation (a) is already known to the receiving party at the time that it is disclosed without use of the Confidential Information; (b) is or becomes publicly known through no wrongful act contrary to this Agreement of the receiving party; (c) is rightfully received from a third party without obligation of confidence or restriction on disclosure from receiving party and without breach of this Agreement; (d) is independently developed by the receiving party without use of Confidential Information; (f) is disclosed pursuant to a requirement of a valid court order provided that the Receiving Party provides (i) prior written notice for the disclosing party of such obligation and (ii) the opportunity to oppose such disclosure and (iii) it is disclosed for the extent and purposes or the order only.
perturbation.changed_text: Information shall not be deemed Confidential Information and the receiving party shall have no obligation regarding any information for which it can be proven with verbal testimony (a) is already known to the receiving party at the time that it is disclosed without use of the Confidential Information; (b) is or becomes publicly known through no wrongful act contrary to this Agreement of the receiving party; (c) is rightfully received from a third party without obligation of confidence or restriction on disclosure from receiving party and without breach of this Agreement; (d) is independently developed by the receiving party without use of Confidential Information; (f) is disclosed pursuant to a requirement of a valid court order provided that the Receiving Party provides (i) prior written notice for the disclosing party of such obligation and (ii) the opportunity to oppose such disclosure and (iii) it is disclosed for the extent and purposes or the order only.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'written documentation' to 'verbal testimony' significantly weakens the standard of proof required to exempt information from confidentiality. This makes it far easier for the receiving party to claim that information is not confidential, even without concrete evidence. This introduces uncertainty in enforcement and contradicts established evidentiary standards for proving prior knowledge.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Evidence Code favors written evidence over oral testimony when proving the content of a writing. By allowing 'verbal testimony' to prove prior knowledge, the contract directly contradicts the established evidentiary preference outlined in the California Evidence Code, making it harder to enforce the confidentiality provisions.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, EVID 1523 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Evidence Code - EVID DIVISION 11. WRITINGS [1400 - 1605] ( Division 11 enacted by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) CHAPTER 2. Secondary Evidence of Writings [1520 - 1567] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) ARTICLE 1. Proof of the Content of a Writing [1520 - 1523] ( Article 1 repealed and added by Stats. 1998, Ch. 100, Sec. 2. ) 1523. (a) Except as otherwise provided by statute, oral testimony is not admissible to prove the content of a writing. (b) Oral testimony of the content of a writing is not made inadmissible by subdivision (a) if the proponent does not have possession or control of a copy of the writing and the original is lost or has been destroyed without fraudulent intent on the part of the proponent of the evidence. (c) Oral testimony of the content of a writing is not made inadmissible by subdivision (a) if the proponent does not have possession or control of the original or a copy of the writing and either of the following conditions is satisfied: (1) Neither the writing nor a copy of the writing was reasonably procurable by the proponent by use of the court’s process or by other available means. (2) The writing is not closely related to the controlling issues and it would be inexpedient to require its production. (d) Oral testimony of the content o
perturbation.justification: The change from 'written documentation' to 'verbal testimony' directly contradicts California Evidence Code EVID 1523(a), which states that 'oral testimony is not admissible to prove the content of a writing' except under specific circumstances. The modified contract weakens the standard of proof required to exempt information from confidentiality, making it easier to claim information is not confidential based on oral testimony, going against the evidentiary preference for written evidence.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information shall remain the property of the disclosing party, and upon the written request of either party, the other party shall promptly return to the disclosing party all Confidential Information disclosed to it and all copies thereof or at the disclosing party's option shall destroy all such Confidential Information and shall provide the receiving party with a certificate that all Confidential Information has been destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information shall remain the property of the disclosing party, and upon the written request of either party, the other party shall promptly return to the disclosing party all Confidential Information disclosed to it and all copies thereof or at the disclosing party's option shall destroy all such Confidential Information and shall provide the receiving party with a verbal confirmation that all Confidential Information has been destroyed.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the requirement of a 'certificate' of destruction to a 'verbal confirmation' weakens the verification process. A certificate provides documentary proof, while verbal confirmation is unreliable and difficult to verify. This creates uncertainty and makes it harder to enforce the return or destruction of confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly mandating written proof in all cases, California law and standard business practices often favor written documentation for important transactions and agreements. Replacing a written certificate with verbal confirmation lowers the standard of proof for destruction of confidential information, potentially making the contract less enforceable in court due to lack of verifiable evidence.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1624 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. MANNER OF CREATING CONTRACTS [1619 - 1633] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) 1624. (a) The following contracts are invalid, unless they, or some note or memorandum thereof, are in writing and subscribed by the party to be charged or by the party’s agent: (1) An agreement that by its terms is not to be performed within a year from the making thereof. (2) A special promise to answer for the debt, default, or miscarriage of another, except in the cases provided for in Section 2794. (3) An agreement for the leasing for a longer period than one year, or for the sale of real property, or of an interest therein; such an agreement, if made by an agent of the party sought to be charged, is invalid, unless the authority of the agent is in writing, subscribed by the party sought to be charged. (4) An agreement authorizing or employing an agent, broker, or any other person to purchase or sell real estate, or to lease real estate for a longer period than one year, or to procure, introduce, or find a purchaser or seller of real estate or a lessee or lessor of real estate where the lease is for a longer period t
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces a 'certificate' with a 'verbal confirmation' for the destruction of confidential information. The Perturbation Explanation highlights this weakens the verification process. Scraped Law Snippet 1, California Code, CIV 1624(a), states that certain contracts are invalid unless they are in writing. While the specific requirement for a certificate of destruction isn't explicitly mandated, the general preference for written documentation in important agreements is evident. The change lowers the standard of proof, potentially making the contract less enforceable. Because the requirement of a certificate is replaced with verbal confirmation, this weakens the contract and creates a legal contradiction based on the provided information.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may be modified only by written amendment signed by both parties.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be modified by any means mutually agreed upon by both parties.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for a 'written amendment signed by both parties' allows for oral modifications, conflicting with the Statute of Frauds and making the contract terms uncertain and difficult to prove. This introduces ambiguity and potential disputes over the actual terms of the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1698 generally requires written modifications to written contracts. By allowing modifications through 'any means,' the contract contradicts this statute, potentially rendering oral modifications unenforceable and creating uncertainty in the contract's interpretation and enforcement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1698 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. EXTINCTION OF CONTRACTS [1682 - 1701] ( Title 5 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. Modification and Cancellation [1697 - 1701] ( Heading of Chapter 3 amended by Stats. 1976, Ch. 109. ) 1698. (a) A contract in writing may be modified by a contract in writing. (b) A contract in writing may be modified by an oral agreement to the extent that the oral agreement is executed by the parties. (c) Unless the contract otherwise expressly provides, a contract in writing may be modified by an oral agreement supported by new consideration. The statute of frauds (Section 1624) is required to be satisfied if the contract as modified is within its provisions. (d) Nothing in this section precludes in an appropriate case the application of rules of law concerning estoppel, oral novation and substitution of a new agreement, rescission of a written contract by an oral agreement, waiver of a provision of a written contract, or oral independent collateral contracts. (Repealed and added by Stats. 1976, Ch. 109.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text, by allowing modifications through 'any means mutually agreed upon,' contradicts California Civil Code § 1698(a), which states that 'A contract in writing may be modified by a contract in writing.' The original text required a 'written amendment,' aligning with the statute. The modified text removes this requirement, opening the door for oral modifications, which are not generally enforceable under the statute unless they meet specific conditions outlined in subsections (b), (c), and (d) (e.g., execution, new consideration, or exceptions like estoppel). Therefore, the change creates a direct contradiction.
